chunk_summary: Guidelines for safe post-discharge fortification of feeds for preterm/LBW/ELBW infants, focusing on stepwise nutrient concentration, hydration monitoring, and preparation safety to prevent complications like dehydration or electrolyte imbalance.
learning_objectives: Understand the stepwise approach to increasing nutrient density in preterm/LBW/ELBW infant feeds.
Learn the core mathematical conversions for calorie density in infant feeding (e.g., kcal/mL).
Recognize the risks of improper fortification, including dehydration and electrolyte derangements.
Identify safe preparation and storage practices for fortified breast milk and formula.
key_facts: Common calorie densities: 20 kcal/oz = 0.67 kcal/mL, 22 kcal/oz = 0.73 kcal/mL, 24 kcal/oz = 0.80 kcal/mL.
Powdered formula is not sterile; prepare with hot water (~70°C) to reduce bacterial risk.
Avoid adding extra formula powder unless a clinician-provided recipe is followed.
Fortified breast milk should be freshly prepared to avoid osmolality increases over time.
Monitor hydration, daily intake (mL/kg/day), and electrolytes after any density change.
reference_quote: "Powdered formula is not sterile; high-risk infants (preterm/LBW) require careful preparation and storage, and hot-water preparation (about 70°C when feasible) reduces bacterial risk." (Reference 1)
document_key_topics: Safe fortification math and calorie density conversions
Stepwise nutrient concentration and hydration monitoring
Preparation and storage safety for fortified feeds
